plan.contextChunk:
how light interacts with surfaces and how those interactions are
recorded with a camera.

## Light Interacting with Surfaces {#sec-light_interacting_with_surfaces}

Visible light is electromagnetic radiation, exhibiting wave effects like
diffraction. For many imaging models, however, it is helpful to
introduce the abstraction of a **light ray**, describing the light
radiation heading in a particular direction from a particular location
in space (@fig-lightSpray). A light ray is specified by its position, direction, and
intensity as a function of wavelength and polarization. In this
treatment, we ignore diffraction effects.

![A light ray from the sun strikes a surface and generates outgoing rays
of intensity and color depending on the angles of the incoming and
outgoing rays relative to the surface orientation.](figures/imaging/brdf.png){#fig-lightSpray width="75%"}

Let an incident light ray be from direction $\mathbf{p}$ and of power,
$\ell_{\texttt{in}}(\lambda)$, as a function of spectral wavelength $\lambda$ ().
The power of the outgoing light, reflected in the direction,
$\mathbf{q}$, is determined by what is called the **bidirectional
reflection distribution function** (BRDF), $F$, of the surface. If the
surface normal is $\mathbf{n}$, then outgoing power is some function,
$F$, of the surface normal, the incoming and outgoing ray directions,
the wavelength, and the incoming light power:
$$\ell_{\texttt{out}} = F \left( \ell_{\texttt{in}}, \mathbf{n}, \lambda, \mathbf{p}, \mathbf{q} \right)
$$ {#eq-Lambert}

### Lambertian Surfaces

In general, BRDFs can be quite complicated (see @Matusik2002),
describing both diffuse and specular components of reflection. Even
surfaces with completely diffuse reflections can give complicated
reflectance distributions @Oren1994. A useful approximation describing
diffuse reflection is the **Lambertian model**, with a particularly
simple BRDF, which we denote as $F_L$. The outgoing ray intensity,
$\ell_{\texttt{out}}$, is a function only of the surface orientation relative to the
incoming and outgoing ray directions, the wavelength, a scalar surface
reflectance, and the incoming light power:
$$
\ell_{\texttt{out}} = F_{L} \left( \ell_{\texttt{in}} (\lambda), \mathbf{n}, \mathbf{p} \right)  = a \ell_{\texttt{in}}(\lambda) \left( \mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{p} \right),
$${#eq-lambert}

where $a$ is the surface **reflectance**, or
**albedo**, $\mathbf{n}$ is the surface normal vector, and $\mathbf{p}$
points toward the source of the incident light. Note that the brightness
of the outgoing light ray depends on the orientation of the surface
relative to the incident ray, as well as the reflectance, $a$, of the
surface. For a Lambertian surface, the intensity of the reflected light
is a function of the direction of the incoming light ray, but not a
function of the outgoing direction of the ray, $\mathbf{q}$.

elements: flat green surface (plane)
incoming colored light ray (from upper left, yellow-orange gradient)
ellipse at intersection point (ray/surface contact)
black surface normal vector n at intersection
incoming ray direction vector p (points along incident ray toward the intersection)
multiple outgoing direction vectors q (black/green arrows in various directions from intersection)
several fainter green arrows around intersection representing alternative outgoing q directions
stylized labels: lin(λ) along the incoming ray, 'n', 'p', 'q' at their vectors, lout = F(lin, n, λ, p, q) above surface

interactions.teaches: Shows how varying the incoming light direction changes the shading according to the surface normal and the dot product n·p in the BRDF definition
Clicking any outgoing q arrow highlights it and displays the resulting outgoing intensity for that direction, illustrating the role of viewing direction in BRDF

demo_steps.title: Incoming light and surface normal
demo_steps.narration: Here, an incoming light ray arrives at the surface. The surface normal n defines the orientation of the surface — it's shown as a black arrow perpendicular to the plane. The incoming ray's direction is marked p, with its spectrum λ and intensity lin(λ) labeled.

demo_steps.title: Outgoing directions and BRDF
demo_steps.narration: At the contact point, several possible outgoing directions q are shown. The outgoing ray intensity lout can depend on both the incoming and outgoing angles. For general surfaces, BRDF describes this dependence mathematically for every pair (p, q).

demo_steps.title: Lambertian: only incoming direction matters
demo_steps.narration: For a Lambertian surface, the outgoing brightness is the same in all directions q, depending only on the angle between n and p — not on where you view from. Try dragging the incoming ray: the more closely p aligns with n, the brighter the reflection in all outgoing directions.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Slightly top-down view, with the green surface as a large rectangle receding back (perspective or shallow tilt), intersection point off-center, incoming ray approaching at an angle from upper left, all arrows and labels visible above the surface
interactionPlan.view_reasoning: (1) The dominant edge is the surface plane, which appears as a horizontally elongated parallelogram receding toward upper right. (2) The baseline plane running left-right and receding means the camera is at a low azimuth (looking roughly along the y-axis toward the top of the image, slightly to the right), so azimuth near 25 deg. (3) The plane shape is a shallow skewed trapezoid, meaning the elevation is moderate (about 30 deg above the plane). The arrows are orthogonal/perpendicular to the surface, supporting this.

camera_view.view_notes: The main surface appears as a wide parallelogram with the intersection point not centered, suggesting a 25 deg azimuth and 30 deg elevation. All major arrows and labels must remain clear in this layout.

inline_constraints.default_view: must match the original silhouette of the surface, intersection point placement, label scale, and whitespace
inline_constraints.visible_ui: no visible sliders/toggles; only drag/click direct manipulation or edge highlights
inline_constraints.explainers: hover tooltips and click popups only for element explanations and formulas
inline_constraints.framing: match the original crop, keeping whitespace above and edges unmutilated
inline_constraints.camera: infer the original's tilt and skew from the surface and vectors; use those as camera targets

interactionPlan.notes: Arrows should update dynamically as the incoming ray is dragged, re-computing their lengths/intensities based on the dot product n·p for the Lambertian case. Outgoing arrows should glow or highlight on click, showing a popup with the current l_out value and the formula. Use transparent colors for the surface, and color gradients for the incoming ray to preserve the visual 'sunbeam' effect.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

evaluation.viewing_angle_source: The source figure shows a large, neon-green rectangular plane oriented in 3D space with its main diagonal running from bottom left to top right, viewed at a shallow angle from above and to the left, such that the lengthwise edge runs from the lower left toward the upper right. The incoming ray enters from the upper left, angled downward, and strikes the plane at a point slightly left of center, with multiple arrows (normals/outgoing) issuing upward and right from that point.
evaluation.viewing_angle_rendered: The rendered screenshot shows a green rectangular plane at a similar shallow angle in perspective, though it appears more foreshortened, and the rectangle is smaller/cropped compared to the source. The incoming ray enters from the top and vertical, hitting the plane's center. The arrows issue from the contact point but are noticeably more vertical.
discrepancies: The camera/viewing angle does not match; the incoming light is vertical rather than diagonal
The incoming light ray is a thick segmented cylinder, not a thin line
Rendered rectangle is too small and too foreshortened, leaving a lot of extra whitespace
All elements are bunched at the center, labels overlap, and the long formula label covers geometry
Text l_in and formula label not placed in same spatial context as source (should be spaced out); p, n, q labels overlap
Missing multiple distinct outgoing arrows (q) in various directions with lighter (more transparent) rendering
evaluation.notes: Plane and arrows present but the camera view, proportions, label positions, and incoming ray primitive are noticeably off; labels overlap.
action_items: Adjust the camera angle, zoom, and target to match the original's above-left view and rectangle scale/crop, including plane orientation.
Use a thin gradient line for the incoming ray instead of a thick segmented cylinder.
Carefully position all labels to avoid overlap and place them where they appear in the source (especially the l_in and formula labels).
Add or accentuate the semi-transparent outgoing arrows to match the number and layout of 'q' directions in the source.
Reduce the whitespace and better center/crop the plane so it closely matches the framing of the source image.

feedback.rationale: The discrepancies and action items focus on camera angle, primitive choice, element scaling, and label placement—concrete rendering and layout issues that can be addressed by refining the generation, while the underlying concept and decomposition are sound.
action_items: Adjust the camera angle, zoom, and target to match the original's above-left view and rectangle scale/crop, including plane orientation.
Use a thin gradient line for the incoming ray instead of a thick segmented cylinder.

Add or accentuate the semi-transparent outgoing arrows to match the number and layout of 'q' directions in the source.
Reduce the whitespace and better center/crop the plane so it closely matches the framing of the source image.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

evaluation.viewing_angle_source: The source figure depicts a planar, neon green quadrilateral surface viewed from above and slightly from the right; the top face of the surface is visible, and the incoming ray, normal, and outgoing directions all emerge from the center. The yellow-orange-red incoming beam enters diagonally from upper left, while outgoing green arrows fan upward/rightward; all arrows and formula labels float above or around the surface patch, which is wide and horizontally oriented.
evaluation.viewing_angle_rendered: The rendered screenshot displays a neon green surface patch foreshortened and seen from slightly above and the right, with the top face visible and the arrows oriented similarly to the source figure. However, the overall zoom is too small, leaving significant whitespace and making the central geometry appear undersized in the composition.

shape_comparison.rendered_shape: thick gradient arrow with triangle head

shape_comparison.source_shape: thick flat arrow (collinear with incoming beam)
shape_comparison.rendered_shape: thick flat arrow (collinear with incoming beam)

discrepancies: Rendered figure is too small with too much whitespace compared to the tightly cropped, wide source.
Some labels overlap central scene content (formulas positioned over arrows).
Font size of all labels is noticeably smaller than in the original.
Missing label for the incoming ray ℓ_in(λ); outgoing formula is misplaced and collides with geometry.
Rendered green color is slightly darker and less saturated than the source's neon tone.
evaluation.notes: All key geometric elements are present and accurately shaped, but crop/zoom are off and labels are too small or collide with scene content.
action_items: Increase scene zoom and adjust camera so the surface and all arrows fill the frame more like the reference crop.
Enlarge label font sizes for all math/symbol text to match source figure prominence.
Carefully reposition labels to avoid overlap with arrows or formula, placing them adjacent to their respective arrows and at matching positions to the source.
Brighten and increase the saturation of the surface green to better match the original neon green tone.
Double-check that all required labels, especially for the incoming light, are present and legible.

feedback.rationale: All geometric and conceptual elements are present and correct, but concrete implementation issues—scene scale, label sizing/positioning, and color—are the sources of discrepancy, which the generator can address without revising the plan.

inline_constraints.visible_ui: no visible sliders/toggles; only drag/click direct manipulation or edge highlights
inline_constraints.explainers: hover tooltips and click popups only for element explanations and formulas
inline_constraints.framing: match the original crop, keeping whitespace above and edges unmutilated
inline_constraints.camera: infer the original's tilt and skew from the surface and vectors; use those as camera targets

interactionPlan.notes: Arrows should update dynamically as the incoming ray is dragged, re-computing their lengths/intensities based on the dot product n·p for the Lambertian case. Outgoing arrows should glow or highlight on click, showing a popup with the current l_out value and the formula. Use transparent colors for the surface, and color gradients for the incoming ray to preserve the visual 'sunbeam' effect.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

evaluation.viewing_angle_source: The source figure is seen from a slightly elevated, oblique angle above the plane, with the plane receding to the right and back. The normal vector rises nearly vertically, and the incoming light ray enters from the left, slanted down toward the contact point. The major text labels sit above the scene, with arrows fanned outward in perspective from the right.
evaluation.viewing_angle_rendered: The rendered screenshot shows the plane at a strong angle, facing away from the viewer and tilted up on the right side. The normal vector rises out from the plane, but the incoming ray and all arrows are projected in a way that causes all figure elements and labels to visually overlap, creating a cluttered, centered stack.

shape_comparison.source_shape: flattened ellipse (circle, viewed at an angle)
black arrow, collinear with incoming ray
shape_comparison.rendered_shape: black arrow, collinear with incoming ray

shape_comparison.source_shape: flat arrows fanned outward, some green faded
shape_comparison.rendered_shape: flat arrows fanned outward, some green faded

shape_comparison.source_shape: large floating serif text, no overlap
shape_comparison.rendered_shape: large floating serif text, but all labels overlap at contact point

discrepancies: All text labels overlap at the contact point causing severe clutter
Plane orientation or camera angle is off, producing an almost edge-on view
Fanned arrows and vectors are visually compressed and lack perspective separation
Major label ℓout=F(…) collides with vectors and p/n/q labels
Overall crop/zoom is tighter than source, losing whitespace
evaluation.notes: All elements exist but crucial perspective/camera and layout errors create overwhelming label clutter and flattened geometry.
action_items: Adjust the camera view to match the source's oblique perspective, with the plane receding right and keeping the normal nearly vertical.
Radically reposition and spread out all labels to prevent overlap, matching the distributed, readable style of the original.
Increase whitespace/crop, reducing the zoom so content does not fill the frame edge-to-edge.
Fan the outgoing q vectors with more visible 3D separation/perspective, as in the source.
Make sure the ℓout = F(...) main label sits well above the geometry and does not collide with arrows or p/n/q points.

feedback.rationale: All core elements and interactions are present and correct, but execution issues like camera angle, label collisions, scale, and inadequate 3D perspective require generator-level layout and rendering fixes rather than a new plan.
